text: During diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon inspects the gastric sleeve, noting a ninety-degree angulation without visible adhesions or scar tissue causing external compression. An endoscope is inserted through the sleeve, and upon insufflation with air, the sleeve visibly assumes a corkscrew appearance with marked angulation at the incisura level.

The surgeon proceeds with the conversion to a gastric bypass. The endoscope is removed after decompressing the sleeve. Dissection is performed along the lesser curve using a blunt technique to create a window behind the gastric sleeve. An endostapler with a blue load is fired to create the gastric pouch, and the distal staple line is oversewn with Vicryl. The transverse mesocolon is reflected, and the ligament of Treitz is identified. The small intestine is measured 125 centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. An endostapler with a white load is fired to create the pancreaticobiliary limb and the Roux limb. The mesentery root is divided using a harmonic scalpel. The Roux limb and biliopancreatic limb are aligned side by side and sutured with an endostitch. Two enterotomies are created using harmonic devices, and an endostapler with a white load is fired to create the jejunojejunostomy anastomosis. The common enterotomy is closed with an absorbable suture in a running manner using an endostitch. A window is created in the transverse mesocolon to position the Roux limb in a retrocolic-retrogastric fashion.

The transverse mesocolon and retromendome are positioned downward, revealing the Roux limb in the left upper quadrant. The Roux limb and gastric pouch are sewn side-to-side, reinforcing the staple line. A gastrotomy and enterotomy, each approximately two centimeters in length, are created using harmonic devices. The surgeon performs the anastomosis by hand, using absorbable sutures, starting with the posterior wall from the distal angle to the proximal end, followed by the anterior wall. An NG tube is placed across the anastomosis to act as a stent. The Roux limb and gastric pouch are sewn in another layer for reinforcement. Air is insufflated to check for leaks, and the mesenteric defect between the Roux limb and transverse mesocolon is sutured using non-absorbable suture material.
